segments.text: Honorable Minister, director General, Dr.
Bramley and everybody else, thank you
for making yourself available this morning.
We have you in here for 30 minutes.
I will allow you to make some opening remarks,
and then we will open it up to your members
for questions. Got it.
Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, yeah,
I will just make a couple of brief comments.
Um, as Associate Minister of Health,
my focus this year has been on delivering practical
improvements across my areas of responsibility,
which include aged care, the development
of a modern regulatory framework for medicines
and medical devices, um, maternal
and women's health, emergency road and air services,
and our smoke-free goal.
Um, but what I want to talk about initially this morning is
just the aged care, um, program of work.
And I'd like to first of all thank the committee
for the work you did in this space
and the production of the report.
Um, obviously there'll be a formal response, um, to
that report, but in, in summary, you have, um,
covered some key areas
and the recommendations are in line with many of the things
that we've been talking about, that I've heard about,
and, um, compliments the direction of travel.
We definitely need more flexibility in the system so
that it provides the help people need.
We need to ensure that the community
and in-home care services are set up the right way so
that people can stay well at home as long
as they can and want to.
And, um, that's the right option.
We need more aged care beds. Absolutely.
We need more respite
and increased capacity to provide specialist dementia,
psychogeriatric, and palliative care,
and for the sector to be funded to provide these services
and invest in building more.
And we need better pathways out
of hospital for older people.
And from what I've seen, that starts
with getting the admission process right
and having upfront specialist advice considering the
individual patient's needs from the outset.
In short, consistent with your report, our system
for aged care needs to be one
where people can access services they need more easily
and transition between these services in a way
that improves health, um, outcomes and quality of life.
These are the issues for the Aged
Care Ministerial Advisory Group.
We'll be working through next year
with this committee's report, part of their consideration.
So thank you, Mr. Chair, and have each take questions.
Thank you, minister Dr. Vel.
Thank you, minister.
Um, and as you noted, the committee's taken a lot of time
to go into the issues around aged care.
You've mentioned the, um, issue of discharge of, um,
elderly people from hospital into aged care.
Can you please, is help us prioritize the top three
high impact actions that can be taken, uh, that,
that you are taking to, uh, uh, speed discharge out
of hospital for people to, to aged care?
I, I think there, there is, um, in terms of actions,
I think there is already actions in place
and we've got some very good programs of work that exist as,
as you'll be aware, um, hospital has a an excellent program
around the admission for through ed.
Um, the Wellington Hospital has an excellent program
around the surgical supporting the surgical unit.
So those are, those are the, um,
priorities I think is the geriatrician, um, in integration
with that process, I think is really important.
The, the, the, you know, actions in terms
of differentiating from BAU around what, what is the, um,
the Ministry and Health New Zealand's program work
around workforce, et cetera.
Those are all the challenges that,
that they will be responding to.
But what we are focused on is, is looking at those, um,
models of excellence that we know exist
and how we can expand those opportunities.
Um, Auckland Hospital also has an excellent program
involving the geriatricians social workers.
Um, and what we've seen is it does produce
excellent results in terms of lowering readmission as well
as shortening hospital stays.
And that's so, so therefore we know those models exist and
therefore it's expanding on that.
Um, having identified them,
do you currently have a program to expand them
that is funded across the country,
or is that under development at the moment
That that is under development?
So this is, um, you know, the,
the individual hospitals working through
what is achievable within their budgets and their processes.
But, um, again, we, we will support those initiatives
as part of this, um, aged care review. Do,
Uh, this is a big issue.
The committee's heard from many submitters on it and,
and when we wrote our report, do you
or your officials who are with you today have a sense
of the resource required to make progress against this,
um, this issue?
We haven't, we haven't got any numbers that I'm aware of.
I'm not sure if they, from an official perspective,
this isn't, um, a, a a separate piece of work
that's securing that this is a priority across all the
sectors because aged care is a, um,
a big consumer of services.
Um, but we have seen significant funding uplift into this,
um, program at work, particularly in the age care sector
Also as resource. Could
you, could you explain the funding please?
So this is for the Aged Care, um, funding uplifts that
To the understood sector.
Alright. And, uh,
you mentioned in your opening re remarks a
ministerial advisory group.
What are you hoping to get out of that group?
So the group, the objective is to have it stood up
by January, um,
to report back no later than the middle of the year.
We, we are expecting it to report back in May.
We've given a very tight timeline, um,
and this is the opportunity to actually look at, you know,
a lot of the recommendations
that have come from your report.
But what we know is that, you know, we have a high demand
of aged care beds,
but the system isn't sustainable as it sits at the moment.
We know that the contracting model isn't
fit for purpose any further.
It's been around for a long time.
So it's a developing system, um, that supports, um,
in-home community care, um, transition
through from hospital, um, aged care
because it's about balancing, you know,
decreasing the demand for aged care beds, as well

So the program of work is, is that system system design
that we're trying to achieve in this space. Mr. One more?
Yeah, thank you. And, um, uh, that sounds very, very, uh,
technical and I'm sure you'll identify good people
to assist you with that technical task.
What should the public expect out of that piece of work?
I think the, the public should, um, ex expect a,
a clearer system design that, I mean, one of the things
that you will have heard a lot through the select committee
is that's lots of complexity around how to access services,
but fundamentally, people want to be able to stay at home
as long as possible.
And I think that's what we should be delivering as a system,
is the opportunity to be supported to live at home as long
as possible, um,
and be able to transition, you know, out of hospital better.
And that's where those, those palliative rehabilitative care
programs would, would complement that.
Thank you Ra, Larry.
Oh, thank you Minister. You've talked about the importance
of keeping people at home,
which is something I think we can all agree on.
In Australia, it was recognized that a critical part of
that workforce or the allied workforce
and, uh, they were having to import workers from overseas
to avoid that there was an uplift of 28.5%
by the Fair Work Commission
for a largely feminized workforce over the last three years.
How can you possibly expect us to be able

claims when your government has done so?
Oh, I think I'd highlight that.
Um, the age care sector is largely
privately delivered, publicly funded,
but privately delivered.
So they have employment relationships with their employers,
so it's a, um, there is still a pay equity claim process
that they can proceed with.
But the other component is that they,
the broadly the recognition is that we need a funding model
that allows the system to be sustainable.
And that's what the focus of this is.
If we have a sustainable funding model, which

and to meet the needs,
what I would say is I've traveled the country a lot over the
last year meeting of aged care providers.
Um, workforce has become less of the issue
that they are managing or challenged by.
Um, what they are challenged by is the funding model, um,
and the, the ability to deliver the service
that is needed within that funding model.
And that's what we're focused on.
We are aware that the pay equity, um, claim
that you mentioned could not happen for a number of years.
So in reality, those pay rates are not going to change.
We have a burned out workforce.
We don't have transparency
because of the private, uh,
public funding model that you've mentioned.
And if I look at the south island,
there are new contracts now that means
that if there are far fewer providers, uh, uh,
and it does mean also the relationships
with those people at home who are being fed
or being showered, very intimate kind of activities,
being given medication each week are
encountering different workers.
Are you satisfied that, uh, your approach to pay equity
and to the continuity of care is the right approach, given
that in reality those pay equity claims will not be able
to be taken for some years?
I'm satisfied that our program work, which is
to improve the funding model
and the contracting environment to ensure
that the system is adequately funded,
is the right work program
Work. And, and final
question on this one. Thank you, minister.
Um, many of those aged care workers are
using their own vehicles.
Um, they're having to travel sometimes on directions given
by people in different cities
who do not understand distances their time for
with their clients, and then they are effectively paying
their own vehicle costs, uh,
which they're not getting reimbursed for.
And the superior report pointed that out.
What have you done to ameliorate that?
Well, there has been, as we've said,
there has been funding uplifts into the aged care sector,
and that's the, the, the delivery of those services.
But I'm not defending the system that you're saying
that this is, there is real inefficiencies about 20% of the,
um, in-home community care budget is spent on travel.
Um, so that, you know, that that is the,
the challenge we've got there is a lot more efficiencies.
There's a lot more opportunity to use innovation
and, um, you know, to create those efficiencies around
how we use the workforce.
Um, and this is why the, the review is underway is to ensure
that we have a fit for purpose, um, contract model
and delivery of service moving forward.
Thank you. Just very, very final one.
If I may, our, our recommendations made it very clear
that if there is to be more investment into the sector,
there needs to be transparency so the money does get
to the workers, to the front line,
and to overcome some of those working conditions such
as the petrol and and so on and costs for workers.
Are you committing that we will have in any reforms,
transparency, that investment will go
to the frontline rather than, uh, go to profit?
The advisory group that we've established is, uh, is those
that are delivering the services,
and that is why we will be ensuring
that we get the right advice around what is the system
that we need to deliver all the expectations,
putting the consumer at the heart of those decisions as to
what is the best experience they can have.
And that is around the, that has been, you know, sort
of outlined in the terms of
reference for this working group. Thank you
Jenny, mark cro. Thank
you. Uh, thank you minister.

Wednesday 3 December 2025 | Health Committee

2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Health

Hon Casey Costello, Minister for Seniors and Associate Minister of Health
